speaker: interviewer
text: Today is August 12, 1996. This survivor is Morris Rich. My name is Bobbi Kaufman. We're in Miami, Florida, in the United States. The language of the interview will be English. Today is August 12, 1996. The survivor being interviewed is Morris Rich. My name is Bobbi Kaufman. We're in Miami, Florida, in United States. The interview will be done in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good afternoon, Mr. Rich.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you do? How do you do? Nice meeting you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Thanks for agreeing to do this today. Could you state your name as it is now?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Morris Rich. R-I-C-H.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Moshe Rutschaisky.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a middle name or any other name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I didn't-- they didn't use my middle name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did your parents call you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maishke.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brothers and sisters called you that as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Maishke.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in school, what name did you use?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maishke. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your language at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Language at birth was actually Yiddish in the house, we were talking.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in public?

speaker: interviewee
text: In public, most Yiddish. Only in school we were talking Lithuanian and Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In April the 20, 1924.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lithuania.

speaker: interviewer
text: What town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kaunas.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you grow up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Slabodka.

speaker: interviewer
text: Explain that to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Slabodka? That's like 10 minutes from Kaunas, only a bridge separated Kaunas from Slabodka.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Slovakia--

speaker: interviewee
text: Slabotka.

speaker: interviewer
text: Slobakia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Slabotka.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that a suburb of Kaunas?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was a suburb, a suburb, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did it look like? What was it like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a very small little town by itself. And Slabodka was very famous, because there was the most biggest yeshivas. People who want to become rabbis used to come to Slabodka yeshivas, to yeshivas, to learn, you know, to become a rabbi. That's why Slabodka was famous all over the world. If you talk to a rabbi in any country, you say you come from Slabodka, they say, ooh, Slabodka. That's the place who became-- big people came out from Slabodka, big rabbis.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you meet any of them? Did you know any of the students?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah, I met a lot. Because a lot of the young people were studying in the Slabodka yeshivas used to rent rooms in my grandmother's house. And she had all the time like five for six boarders, and they were all what you call yeshiva bocherim. They were all studying to become rabbis.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: These students?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they had already finished their early education?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They finish their early education, and then they went to become rabbis.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to Talmud Torah. It's called that school, Talmud Torah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this in the same town?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the same town, and it was more like a religious school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your day like at school?

speaker: interviewee
text: We learned, you know, Lithuanian, history, geography, and a lot of Hebrew and religious learnings.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get to school each day? Did you walk?

speaker: interviewee
text: Walked, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about lunch? Did you go home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lunch, my mother used to give me in a little package, in a bag, in a little paper bag. And I used to eat the lunch that my mother used to give it to me to take it to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me that your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Family? Well, very devoted parents. My father, my mother exceptionally devoted. Because God forbid a child got a little fever, you know? Like they're calling in this country a 99 and 100, she used to fall in a panic. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she call the doctor?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My mother was a doctor by herself. She had already all the remedies. [LAUGHS] But she learned in all-- only in dangerous situations she called a doctor.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were some of her remedies? What did she do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Old fashioned remedies, you know, like certain herbs, you know. Certain things, you know. Like now we are-- like they start now in the United States with vitamins. My mother knew already many years ago, certain things what makes you feel better. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hodel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Flink Hodel Flink

speaker: interviewer
text: When was she born?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was born in 1900.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lithuania.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chaim, his first name, Rutschaisky.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell Rutschaisky for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that's-- I'm going to give you a good job now. It's R-U-T-S-C-H-A-I-S-K-Y.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was he born?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born Lithuania, in Lithuania.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how your parents met?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think my mother told me that she visited her girlfriend, and her girlfriend was married already at that time to a tailor. And my father knew the tailor. They were friends with the tailor. And that's how they met.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you have brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brothers and sisters? They were the same thing, going to school with me together.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother's name is-- my oldest sister's name is Faiga. My brother's name is Abraham. And my younger sister's name is Miriam. And my little brother who got killed in the crematorium with my mother, his name was Solomon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what your house looked like.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was houses like almost like what they build in-- in the Carolinas, from logs, wooden logs, but chopped wooden logs. And it was a warm-- special build like that to keep warm. And inside it was wallpaper and beautiful, and it was very comfortable.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe it a little more.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We didn't have central air conditioning or central heat. We had stoves. And we had in the wintertime to take chopped wood, and keep on heating up the ovens to keep the whole house warm. But it was a very comfortable, warm house. And outside the house we had a lot of big fields. And people grew in the summertime tomatoes, cucumbers. And all the time when we looked out, we saw green. Because we lived actually in the outskirts of Slabodka. We didn't live right in the heart of Slabodka. We lived already outside. Because they started to build factories and houses around there. And we moved there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you have a yard?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We had a yard. And we had a place, a little to play, even a little football too, soccer.

speaker: interviewer
text: You liked soccer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That's was my main-- my main game was soccer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, did you play on a team, or how did you play?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We played here, the whole team from Talmud Torah. And I was-- not to praise myself-- I was very fast. [LAUGHS] And I played all the time, the outside right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people come watch your games? Did your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Sometimes, yeah. They did. But I played too much soccer. My mother used to get angry because sometimes I didn't come on time for dinner. And she used to say, yeah, he's playing already soccer again, that football. That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else did you do as a kid?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ping pong we played, a lot of ping pong. And in the wintertime, we lived not far what is called the [? Linkuva ?] mountain, big hills. And we had sleds. And we used to sled down from the mountains, which is involved in a little danger. But be did it. That was our winter sport, to go to that [? Linkuva ?] mountain, and used to be the sleds to go down.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a woodturner.

speaker: interviewer
text: What exactly does that mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: To make you laugh, he takes a square piece of wood and he makes it round. But that's not all. I'm just making a joke. A woodturner-- a woodturner is who makes parts for furniture, architectural, and industrial, anything what's got to do with shaping out the wood. And people have to make casting to make metal, or build a home with fancy columns, or use spools for the textile industry. My father had to supply all these type turnings. Some of the big textile companies needed very precision spools. So when the machines run, this spool was not little spools like you'd buy in the store. It was commercial spools, almost you know, a foot tall, and by 6 inches in diameter. Build almost, you know, like a tapered bottle. And these spools had to go very precision. Otherwise the silk or the cotton tears, so it's got to run very good. And my father was actually a very precision, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] excellent woodturner.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you learn with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to keep an eye. He didn't want me to learn-- teach me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because woodturning was a poor trade, even the specialties and precision. But all trades in my country, turnings, woodturning, tailors, shoemakers, all trades were hardly making a good living. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did he hope for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: He worked hard, and he was very good in his trade. So he made a better living than other woodturners. Because he was more actually like educated woodturner. He knew how to read blueprints. And he was a self-educated person. He used to read a lot of books. And he knew how to complete a job according to the blueprint. And that's why he had more jobs than other woodturners, who did only, you know like, furniture turnings. My father did a lot of turnings for industrial too. Before you make a mold, for iron or parts for machinery, it had to be made first in wood, turned. And then it could be casted. And that had to be turned according to the blueprint. And my father was very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: You must have learned something from him.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, I learned. I used to sneak in, in the shop. And he used to go to a customer to deliver, and play around with his tools. But he recognized when he came back, some of the tools was a little chipped. He says, somebody was already in the shop. Who was in the shop?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they know it was you? Were you the little bad boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I was a bad boy. So he didn't want me to go in the shop for one reason. He says, when you start going to the shop, you'll become a woodturner. And there is better things than woodturning. And that's why he didn't like me to go into the shop.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said there were books in your house.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We had all different types. My father was a well-read person. He knew everything, what was going on in this world.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know what was happening at the beginning of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My father knew that the war is going to come. Because he didn't only read the Jewish newspapers or Lithuanian newspapers, he even read And according to this, he told all the time my mother, we don't have a chance to hang around Lithuania. And he knew exactly what is going to be. But my mother didn't want to believe. You know, nobody wants to believe in bad things that's going to come out. But he knew, you know, Hitler's plan. And when he started to take all the other countries, you know, Czechoslovakia, and he starts with the war in Poland, you know. But before the war in Poland, he begged my mother. He says, let's sell the house. Let's sell the shop. Let's get out. Let's go to my family in the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the United States was my all father's family most, the brothers. He had two-- he had three sisters in the United States. And they left many years before the war, lucky. They left in the right time. But my mother all the time contradicted him. She said, oh, Chaim, you're a woodturner. Look at the big manufacturers, the big names, [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], the big textile. Actually Jewish tycoon is what you call it in Slabodka. But they had nice factories.

speaker: interviewer
text: They are building the factories larger. And they don't leave Lithuania. So you are the philosopher? You are the only smart man? You want to run? He says, it's going to be bad, very serious, all standing on an iceberg which is melting under our feet. And it's going to melt. And we are all going to be sunk, and drowned, and dead, and murdered. Do you actually remember these conversations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I remember all the time. Because we were kids, and we used to say, yeah, yeah, [? Papa, ?] let's go to America. Let's go. Let's go. Let's go. But, you know what happens, you know. My father was a good husband. And he didn't want to push too hard. But when the war broke out, my mother cried bitter tears. And she said, bitterly crying, look, she says. Chaim, your father, how right he was. Look. Look at exactly-- exactly what he said, exactly the outcome what it is. Because Hitler had systematically, you know, done what was right in his you know. All, you know, organized, you know, planned out to kill out all the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, let's go back. I want to ask you a few more things. Then we'll get to this.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah? Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you practice your Judaism in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, I was going to the Talmud Torah, salient all the time. 40% of the education in Talmud Torah was Hebrew and the Bible, and Tanakh and [? Chumash, ?] you know, all the Jewish laws, and a little Gemara. So I knew already, you know, about [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the Jewish, you know, traditions and the Judaism, you know. And my mother was keeping, of course, her house. All the people in Slabodka they are kosher.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there non-Jews in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. A lot of non-Jews. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you friends with them? Did you interact?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, We were with some of them, we were very friendly. And it turned out when the war starts we didn't know we are not friendly. Because we had very big disappointments. Because some of our friendly people who were our neighbors, came in right away to look for my father to kill him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were Lithuanians, you know, gentile people. And they were preparing for years, if something comes up and they start a war with Poland, and then the Russians in 1940, took Lithuania, and all the Baltic countries, you know, after that pact with Russia. You see, Germany and Russia made a pact. And the Russians got certain countries, like a gift, for cutting in, you know with Hitler that peace, so-called pact. As it was, you know, later nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Lithuanians already prepare themselves with rifles and with weapons, digging in, right in their yards. And I, myself, came in one time to my father. I says, what is Mazuras, one of our neighbors, Mr. Mazuras, what is he digging all the time and working in his yard?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father says, I don't know. I hope he doesn't bury weapons there. Because we had a feeling that he had-- he was like an organizer. A lot of Lithuanian gentile people used to come to his home. And the fact is, anytime you know, the telephone was ringing in our house, customers, because we had a telephone, and he didn't have a telephone. So when people used to call for him, I used to come into his house and said, Mr. Mazuras, you are called to the telephone. And we were nice to him all these years. But not after the-- the war broke out. After the war broke out, that same man, Mazuras, gave out all [INAUDIBLE] gentile Lithuanians the weapons. And they got their first ones after six days, when the Germans marched in into Lithuania, did a pogrom in Slabodka. He didn't kill my mother or our children, but he came to kill my father. And my father was already killed. Because he was in the first day, he was going to see if the train is going, and the Lithuanians, already partisans they called themselves, grabbed him right by the train, and they took him away to the Ninth Fort with a lot of other Jewish men, and they killed him later.

speaker: interviewer
text: After the time that the Soviet Union took over Lithuania, that was in 1940?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. May, they marched in May.

speaker: interviewer
text: How how did life change right away then?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a big change, of course. It's-- for the rich people who had the textile factories and all the big enterprises, it was not too happy, because it was right away taken away by the Russians. And it was made, you know, cooperative factories. But for the poor people who didn't have anything to lose, the Russians-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they were against religion, of course. So the religious schools were already starting to change. And it was a change, in general, you know, when the Russians came in. It was not a happy outcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the pogroms started then?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, by the Russians it was not pogroms. The Russians just took away from rich people properties, and they wanted to-- everybody to become a Communist. But not everybody wanted to be a Communist.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it's already, you know, a-- a problem causing, you know. But it was no killings by Russian soldiers or Russians to kill Jews. That began when the Germans invaded?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the Germans marched in.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your neighbor took part in this pogrom?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened? He came into your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: He came into the house. Yes. And he says, where is Chaim? My mother says, Chaim is not here. We don't know where he is. We didn't know yet that my father was killed. He says, you all can be in your house. Nothing's going to happen to you. But I must find Chaim. And he had a hate against my father. He didn't find Chaim, so he just walked out from the house. And he says, I'm going to tell my partisans not to touch your house. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But he went, and he killed plenty Jews in other sections.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I imagine you knew these Jewish families.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. We knew a lot of families.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you all communicate with each other? What--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was hard to communicate after the war broke out already. Because walking from one place to the other, your life was in danger. Because the Lithuanians, when the war broke out, they were the first killers. Before even the Germans marched in, the Lithuanians already, they're shooting and killing Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who armed the Lithuanians?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Lithuanians.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they get their arms, their ammunition?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were preparing already for years in the underground.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to stop for a minute now.
